evidence: The build establishes the two number columns, then the lilac score readout counts up as the focal payoff; in later frames the columns recede in brightness while the score and question card carry focus.
Elements are introduced incrementally across frames — columns, then values, then connectors and score, then the question card — never more than four novel ideas at once.
The 'your taste' and 'this song' labels sit directly atop their number columns and 'score' sits beside its readout; no legend or corner key exists.
The only prose is the question card 'How do you score two lists of numbers?' which annotates the visible paired columns and score readout beside it.
All text — column labels, numeric values, score, and question card — is well above the ~2.2% frame-height legibility threshold.
Every element sits fully inside the frame with comfortable margins; nothing is cropped.
Columns, connectors, score, and question card occupy distinct regions with no overlapping or intersecting text.
Both paired number columns wear periwinkle (observed data) and the score readout is lilac, consistent with the intent and token semantics.
Every element — labels, numbers, connector lines, score, question card — carries propositional content; no decoration present.

The two side-by-side columns slide in, paired entries connect in periwinkle, the lilac score counts up to 0.87, and the question card holds over the frozen score with half the frame empty — matching the intent.
Paired values sit adjacent with connecting lines and the score sits directly beneath its columns; the question card sits in the deliberately empty right half.
Across frames the diagram builds in reasoning order — labels, then values, then connectors and score, then the question card — as a continuous construction.
Generous negative space, deliberate empty right half, and clear left-to-right reading order give the composition ample breathing room.
